title: Choosing the Right Activation Function
concept: A decision matrix reviewing every activation function covered so far — Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU, Swish/GELU, and Softmax — and when to reach for each one.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what does it mean for Swish and GELU to be self-gated?

options.text: Each input is scaled by a gate calculated from that same input's own value
They gate access to the training data
They only run during the warmup phase

exercises.explain: Right — self-gated functions scale themselves. You've now met eight activation functions total. Time to organize the whole toolbox.

cards.title: Eight tools, one toolbox
cards.body: Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU, Swish, GELU, Softmax — that's a lot of activation functions to keep straight. The good news: they sort into just a few clear groups, each built for a specific job.

cards.title: Four groups, one purpose each
cards.body: Squashing functions (Sigmoid, Tanh) compress numbers into a fixed range. The ReLU family (ReLU, Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU) chops or bends negative inputs while leaving positive ones alone. Smooth modern functions (Swish, GELU) self-gate for a small edge in large networks. And Softmax stands alone as the output-layer specialist for picking one winner among many categories.

cards.title: A toolbox, not one favorite wrench
cards.body: A carpenter doesn't use one tool for every job — a hammer for nails, a screwdriver for screws, a saw for cutting. Activation functions work the same way: ReLU is the reliable everyday hammer for hidden layers, Sigmoid and Softmax are precision tools reserved for the output layer, and Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU, Swish, and GELU are specialty tools you reach for when the everyday hammer isn't quite doing the job.

cards.title: A quick decision guide
cards.body: Hidden layers, general default: reach for ReLU. Seeing dying neurons? Try Leaky ReLU, PReLU, or ELU. Building a large modern network and chasing every bit of performance? Try Swish or GELU. Output layer, single yes/no decision: Sigmoid. Output layer, one winner among many categories: Softmax. Output layer, hidden-layer-style bend needed for a two-direction signal: Tanh.

cards.title: There's no single 'best' activation function
cards.body: You might assume one of these eight must be objectively the best, and the rest are outdated. In reality, the right choice depends on where it sits in the network (hidden vs. output layer) and what problem it's solving — a specialist for multi-class output isn't a competitor to a hidden-layer default, they solve different jobs entirely.

left.text:
ReLU, Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU, Swish, GELU
Process signals between layers

right.text:
Sigmoid (yes/no), Softmax (one of many)
Produce the network's final answer

exercises.prompt: A hidden layer keeps producing dead neurons stuck at 0. Which group of activation functions was specifically built to fix this?
exercises.explain: Right — these three all keep a signal alive for negative inputs instead of hard-zeroing them.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Softmax and Sigmoid can be swapped for each other with no real difference, since both squash numbers into a 0-to-1 range.
exercises.explain: False! Sigmoid scores one independent decision; Softmax links a whole set of categories together so they sum to 100%.

exercises.sentence: For a network's output layer choosing one winner among many categories, the right activation function is ___.
exercises.explain: Softmax is the output-layer specialist for multi-class problems.

exercises.prompt: A large modern network wants a smooth, self-gated hidden-layer activation function for a small performance edge. Which should it use?
exercises.explain: Swish and GELU are the smooth, self-gated choices built for exactly this.

exercises.prompt: You're designing a network with hidden layers that have been suffering from dying neurons, plus an output layer that must pick exactly one winner among 20 categories. Which activation function choices fit both jobs, and why?

options.text: Leaky ReLU, PReLU, or ELU for the hidden layers (they keep negative-input signals alive instead of hard-zeroing them), and Softmax for the output layer (it converts scores into probabilities across all 20 categories that sum to 100%)
ReLU everywhere, because the same default always works best no matter the problem
Sigmoid for the hidden layers and Tanh for the output layer
Softmax for the hidden layers and ReLU for the output layer

exercises.explain: Exactly right! You now know all eight activation functions and when to reach for each. But knowing how a neuron reacts to a signal is only half the story — next, you'll learn HOW a network actually adjusts its weights to get better over time, starting with the algorithm that powers nearly all of deep learning: gradient descent.
